dialogue.utterance: Unit 3 responding to a female for abdominal pain. Patient is a 53-year-old in Richmond.
Hi ma'am, I'm Alex with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've had abdominal pain-left and right lower quadrants. Also chest discomfort and shortness of breath when resting. It started yesterday and is worse now.
Linda, can you tell me your name for us?
My name is Linda.
Can you tell me where we are right now?
I'm in Richmond. What about me?
Quickly looking in your mouth to check your airway. Is that okay?
Yes. I've already had a look done; it's fine.
Linda, can you take a deep breath in and out while I check your lungs please.
Lungs are clear in all fields. Has your breath ever felt difficult while doing this or at all?
Let me check your pulse and baseline circulation, Linda.
Pulse is 101, regular and strong.
Blood pressure is 168 over 91. SpO₂ on room air is 94.
Respiratory rate is 16 with normal depth.
Let me check your glucose with a finger prick.
Blood sugar is 510-high, but stable for now.
When did the abdominal discomfort and chest pain start first?
It started yesterday and has waxed and waned-but today it got worse. My blood sugar's high too.
What were you doing when the pain began yesterday?
I was just at home, resting. Had been in the hospital a week ago with the same symptoms.

Do you have a past medical history, like heart problems or diabetes? 23. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: pertinent_past_medical_history; Patient: I had a heart attack, have diabetes, high blood pressure, and hyperkalemia. I also have anxiety. 24. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: medications; EMT: Can you list your current medications please? 25. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: medications; Patient: I'm on Lantus, NovoLog, Levoxyl, Coreg, Plavix, Zoloft, Lorazepam, Gabapentin, Iron, Multivitamin, and Sertraline.
Do you have any known allergies to meds or treatments?
I am allergic to Fentanyl (Duragesic), Tramadol, Toradol, Ketorolac, Airmet, and Nitroglycerin.
Can you tell me about your eating and drinking recently? 29. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: last_oral_intake; Patient: I eat small portions. I haven't been very hungry lately.
When exactly did the pain first begin, and when did it change its pattern?
It began yesterday, and it's more severe today. The character seems different too.
How do you describe the pain? Is it a stabbing, burning, or some other type?
A dull or sharp pain. It's hard to place a form on it.
What aggravates it? Movement? Breathing?
When I lie down it sharpens. When I sit, it eases somewhat.
Does the pain move to other parts of your body, like your chest?
It sometimes radiates up to my chest-makes it hard to breathe.
On a scale of 0 to 10, can you rate how bad your pain is right now? 39. Pain Assessment; O.P.Q.R.S.T.: severity; Patient: It's a 10.
How often is the pain occurring, or is it constant?
It's constant, but the sensation changes when I'm at rest or moving.
Can I look at your skin for any cardiac or injury signs?
Yes.
There are bruises in your lower left and right abdominal regions. When did these appear?
A week ago after my hospital stay. They don't seem to be getting worse.
Linda, I will now do a full body assessment for any deformities, contusions, or abrasions, per DCAP-BTLS.
No significant findings noted. Skin is warm, pink, and dry. No rashes or bites.
I'll now check your head and neck. This is part of a full secondary exam.
Okay.
No signs of injury or swelling in the head or neck area now.
Your lung sounds and chest sounds are normal.
I'll apply a 4-lead ECG now. It helps monitor any lateral ischemia findings.
Sure.
I'm going to gently press in different areas of your abdomen. Let me know if that's painful.
Yes, that is painful.
Pressing now.
Very painful.
May I check your legs and back for any signs of injury or fall risks?
Yes.
I can't find any injuries or deformities. Peripheral pulses are strong.
Blood pressure is 156 over 89. Pulse still at 97 and steady.
Respiratory rate is 19 and normal depth. No distress noted.
SpO₂ is now 95% on room air.
Quick glucose check is at zero-no change in values.
I understand we can start an IV for fluid access and medication capabilities. Is that okay?
Go ahead.
Access has been placed in your forearm through an extremity vein.
We'll give you some oxygen to help with your shortness of breath. Does that feel better?
Yes, it feels a bit more manageable.
BP is 163 over 78 now. SpO₂ remains at 95%.
Breathing is 20, normal depth. Everything's still stable.
Pulse is 96 and steady. We're continuing close observation.
Need to remeasure your glucose before proceeding. Can you prick your finger please?
Glucose is still at zero. SpO₂ remains at 95%. No change yet.
On the same 0 to 10 scale, how is your pain now?
It's still a 10 in the abdomen, but the chest discomfort eased some.
Has the pain expanded at all, or remained in the same place?
It remains in the same area, but the chest part is intermittent.
Pulse is 97 and steady now.
Respiration is at 20 with no new signs of distress.
SpO₂ has improved to 96%.
We'll increase your oxygen via nasal cannula for better support.
Which hospital would you prefer for transport today?
I prefer Johnston-Willis.
We'll place you on the stretcher now. Is a 5-point harness okay for your transport safety?
Yes, be careful with me.
You're in good hands, Linda. Hospital is aware of our approach.
We are now traveling to Johnston-Willis Hospital. Any other concerns during transport?
I'm tired but okay for now. Monitor as you go.
Blood pressure is 163 over 96. Now, stable again.
SpO₂ is at 96%. Positive trend.
Pulse is now 97 with no irregularities, rhythm steady again.
Respiratory rate is 18 with normal breaths.
It's a documented protocol for patients who have MI history. Just now, I administered aspirin.
All steps are in place for safe transport to the hospital. ED staff are notified.
I trust you will make good care of me.
We are now arriving at the hospital. ED team is prepared.
ED team will receive the patient, and handover is proceeding as directed.
GCS is 15, alert and oriented, SpO₂ at 97%, stable and ready for ED care.
The 5-point harness is on, patient is secured, and on final transport.
